text:
In this study, a library of novel bis-Schiff base derivatives incorporating a thiobarbituric acid scaffold was synthesized via a multi-step reaction pathway as part of an effort to identify potent anti-diabetic agents. The structures of the synthesized compounds were elucidated using HR-ESI-MS and NMR ($^{1}\text{H}$ and $^{13}\text{C}$) spectroscopy. All derivatives were evaluated for their *in vitro* $\alpha$-glucosidase inhibitory activity, with the majority demonstrating superior potency compared to the clinical standard, acarbose ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.27 \pm 0.04\ \mu\text{M}$). Notably, compounds **8** ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.10 \pm 0.05\ \mu\text{M}$) and **9** ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.13 \pm 0.03\ \mu\text{M}$), featuring electron-withdrawing chlorine substituents, emerged as the most effective inhibitors. 

To explore the structure-activity relationships and molecular mechanisms of inhibition, molecular docking simulations were conducted, revealing strong binding affinities and key stabilizing interactions within the enzyme's active site. Furthermore, density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level were employed to analyze the electronic properties and geometric stability of the hybrids. The existence of intramolecular hydrogen bonding was confirmed through Reduced Density Gradient (RDG) and DFT-D3 analysis, while Frontier Molecular Orbital (FMO) analysis and Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MEP) mapping provided insights into the chemical reactivity and nucleophilic/electrophilic sites of the compounds. These findings suggest that these thiobarbituric acid-based bis-Schiff bases serve as promising lead scaffolds for the development of advanced therapeutic agents for the management of type 2 diabetes.
